title: Peripheral Arterial Disease
oneLiner: Atherosclerotic narrowing of leg arteries causing claudication or critical ischaemia; major marker of systemic vascular risk.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute limb ischaemia (embolus / thrombosis):** The leg is cold, pale, pulseless, paralysed, paraesthetic – this is a vascular emergency. Single finding: **sudden loss of pulses + motor deficit** (inability to wiggle toes) = irreversible ischaemia within 6 hours.
- **Ruptured AAA (mimic):** Back/flank pain with shock, but also can present as unilateral leg with pulselessness due to dissection/compression. Single finding: **palpable pulsatile abdominal mass + haemodynamic instability** – do not let leg findings distract you.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Intermittent claudication: calf/buttock/thigh pain on walking, relieved by rest within minutes
  - Critical limb ischaemia (CLI): rest pain (especially at night, relieved by hanging leg down), non-healing ulcer, gangrene
  - Acute limb ischaemia: 6 P's (Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paraesthesia, Paralysis, Perishing cold)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Reduced or absent pulses (femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial)
  - Buerger's test: pallor on elevation, rubor on dependency
  - Capillary refill > 3 seconds
  - Muscle wasting / hair loss / shiny skin
  - Ulcer location: typically toes, heel, or over bony prominences (distal, punched out, pale base)
  - Auscultate for bruits over femoral and popliteal arteries
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Peripheral arterial disease (PAD)** – intermittent claudication, pulses absent, ABPI < 0.9
- **Neurogenic claudication (spinal stenosis)** – bilateral, relieved by leaning forward, pulses present, ABPI normal
- **Chronic venous insufficiency** – ulcers medial malleolus, varicose veins, oedema, pulses present
- **Musculoskeletal leg pain** – pain at rest as well as on movement, no vascular risk factors
- **Deep vein thrombosis** – acute swelling, warmth, positive ultrasound, ABPI not relevant
- **Acute compartment syndrome** – post-trauma/ischaemia, tense compartment, severe pain on passive stretch
- **Splitter:** **Does the pain resolve within 2–3 minutes of rest?** Yes → PAD. No → neurogenic claudication (needs longer to settle). Plus ABPI < 0.9 confirms PAD.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI): >1.4 suggests non-compressible (often diabetes); 0.9–1.4 normal; 0.5–0.9 claudication; < 0.5 CLI; acute limb ischaemia: often inaudible doppler signal
  - Toe pressures / transcutaneous oxygen (TCPO2) if ABPI unreliable (e.g. diabetes)
  - ECG (look for atrial fibrillation as embolic source)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, eGFR, LFTs, lipid profile, HbA1c, coagulation (if anticoagulation considered)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Duplex ultrasound** – localises stenosis/occlusion, guides intervention
  - Second-line: CT angiography (CTA) – for surgical/endovascular planning; best for multi-level disease
  - MR angiography (if contra-indication to CT contrast)
- **Special tests:**
  - Treadmill exercise test for claudication distance (if needed)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Rutherford classification** (0 = asymptomatic, 1–3 = claudication, 4 = rest pain, 5–6 = minor/major tissue loss)
  - **WIfI** (Wound, Ischaemia, foot Infection) for CLI staging
- **Never-miss test:** **ABPI + duplex ultrasound** in any patient > 50 with leg pain or non-healing ulcer – missing PAD means missing risk of MI/stroke.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Acute limb ischaemia:**
    - Immediate IV heparin (5000 IU bolus then 1000 IU/hour, check local protocol) – do not wait for imaging if pulses absent
    - **Urgent vascular surgeon review** – time is muscle
    - NBM, IV fluids, analgesia, ECG, bloods (including troponin)
    - If viable (no motor loss): urgent CTA; if non-viable (paralysis > 4hrs): amputation likely
  - **Chronic symptomatic PAD:** no acute rush
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start antiplatelet (clopidogrel 75 mg OD preferred, or aspirin 75 mg OD)
  - High-intensity statin: atorvastatin 80 mg OD (target LDL < 1.4 mmol/L)
  - Optimise blood pressure (target < 140/90, or < 130/80 if diabetes/chronic kidney disease)
  - Diabetes control
  - Smoking cessation advice + referral to cessation service
  - Supervised exercise programme referral (NICE recommends 3 months)
  - Wound care (if ulcer: keep clean, dry, non-compressive dressings; refer to podiatry)
  - Consider revascularisation: endovascular (angioplasty/stent) or surgical (bypass) depending on anatomy
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Continue antiplatelet + statin + risk factor management
  - Foot care advice: daily inspection, moisturise, avoid trauma, appropriate footwear
  - Repeat ABPI at 6–12 months or if symptoms worsen
  - Surveillance if bypass/stent – duplex ultrasound at 1, 6, 12 months then yearly
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV normal saline 1 L over 8h (if NBM for procedure) or oral hydration
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (provided not anticoagulated already)
  - Diet: NBM if planned angiogram/surgery; otherwise normal diet
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2; if acute ischaemia, hourly for first 6h
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Clopidogrel:** 75 mg PO OD (first line for symptomatic PAD)
  - **Aspirin:** 75 mg PO OD (if clopidogrel not tolerated)
  - **Atorvastatin:** 80 mg PO OD (high intensity, regardless of baseline LDL)
  - **Cilostazol:** 100 mg PO BD – for claudication when symptoms persist despite exercise, only if no heart failure (contraindicated in HF). Dose: 100 mg BD
  - **Naftidrofuryl oxalate:** 200 mg PO TDS – alternative for claudication if cilostazol not suitable
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BD + aspirin 100 mg OD (for high-risk patients, post-revascularisation? check local policy)
  - Anti-hypertensives: ACEi (ramipril 2.5-10 mg OD) or ARB if ACEi intolerant
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Cilostazol: contraindicated in heart failure (any NYHA class)
  - Clopidogrel + PPI (esomeprazole) – weak interaction; use pantoprazole if needed
  - Statins: avoid in severe liver disease; check LFTs before start
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Atorvastatin: no dose adjustment in mild-moderate renal impairment; caution if eGFR < 30
  - Clopidogrel: no adjustment
  - Cilostazol: avoid if eGFR < 25 mL/min
  - [check local policy] for heparin dosing in renal impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Heparin in acute limb ischaemia:** forgetting to give the bolus – leads to extension of thrombosis. Always check aPTT 6 hours after start. If critical limb ischaemia, consider weight-based dosing. Also **heparin + antiplatelet** – risk of bleeding; monitor for haematomas.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Acute limb ischaemia** (embolus/thrombus) – risk of amputation, compartment syndrome, reperfusion injury (hyperkalaemia, acidosis, myoglobinuria – treat with fluids, sodium bicarbonate, mannitol per local protocol)
  - **Rhabdomyolysis** from severe ischaemia – check CK, urine myoglobin
  - **Bleeding** from antiplatelet/anticoagulation (especially if dual therapy)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Non-healing ulcer / gangrene** – amputation (major/minor)
  - **Cardiovascular events** (MI, stroke, CV death) – PAD is a strong marker of systemic atherosclerosis
  - **Impotence** (aorto-iliac disease)
  - **Infection** in ischaemic ulcer (cellulitis, osteomyelitis)
sections.Prognosis: - 5-year risk of major cardiovascular events (MI/stroke) is 20-30% in symptomatic PAD. Up to 30% of those with critical limb ischaemia undergo major amputation within 1 year. Risk factor modification + revascularisation reduce amputation risk.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always calculate ABPI yourself at the bedside** – the most accessible test.
- **Check for atrial fibrillation** in all acute limb ischaemia – embolic source.
- **Refer all smokers to a smoking cessation service** – single best intervention for symptom progression.
- **Ensure foot inspection daily** – especially in diabetes and end-stage PAD.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not prescribe compression stockings** in arterial disease – they worsen ischaemia.
- **Do not assume leg pain is arthritis** in an elderly smoker with diabetes – check ABPI.
- **Do not delay heparin in acute limb ischaemia** while waiting for imaging – muscle necrosis begins within 4-6 hours.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden worsening of leg pain at rest
  - Leg becomes cold, pale, or numb
  - New foot ulcer develops or existing ulcer enlarges / becomes black
  - Chest pain, breathlessness, or syncope (possible MI)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Vascular clinic within 2 weeks for claudication (or 1 week for CLI)
  - GP for cardiovascular risk review (lipids, BP, glucose) in 4 weeks
  - Supervised exercise programme referral (if applicable)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / vascular registrar immediately if:**
  - Acute limb ischaemia with motor loss (paralysis)
  - NEWS2 > 5 or lactate > 4 mmol/L
  - Suspected ruptured AAA (shock + pulsatile mass)
  - Compartment syndrome (tense leg, pain on passive stretch, elevated compartment pressure)
  - Any significant bleeding (e.g. retroperitoneal haematoma after intervention)
- **ICU criteria:** critical ischaemia with multi-organ failure, severe reperfusion syndrome, or post-major amputation with haemodynamic instability.

rawMarkdown:
## Peripheral Arterial Disease
**One-liner:** Atherosclerotic narrowing of leg arteries causing claudication or critical ischaemia; major marker of systemic vascular risk.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute limb ischaemia (embolus / thrombosis):** The leg is cold, pale, pulseless, paralysed, paraesthetic – this is a vascular emergency. Single finding: **sudden loss of pulses + motor deficit** (inability to wiggle toes) = irreversible ischaemia within 6 hours.
- **Ruptured AAA (mimic):** Back/flank pain with shock, but also can present as unilateral leg with pulselessness due to dissection/compression. Single finding: **palpable pulsatile abdominal mass + haemodynamic instability** – do not let leg findings distract you.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Intermittent claudication: calf/buttock/thigh pain on walking, relieved by rest within minutes
  - Critical limb ischaemia (CLI): rest pain (especially at night, relieved by hanging leg down), non-healing ulcer, gangrene
  - Acute limb ischaemia: 6 P's (Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paraesthesia, Paralysis, Perishing cold)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Reduced or absent pulses (femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial)
  - Buerger's test: pallor on elevation, rubor on dependency
  - Capillary refill > 3 seconds
  - Muscle wasting / hair loss / shiny skin
  - Ulcer location: typically toes, heel, or over bony prominences (distal, punched out, pale base)
  - Auscultate for bruits over femoral and popliteal arteries

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Peripheral arterial disease (PAD)** – intermittent claudication, pulses absent, ABPI < 0.9
- **Neurogenic claudication (spinal stenosis)** – bilateral, relieved by leaning forward, pulses present, ABPI normal
- **Chronic venous insufficiency** – ulcers medial malleolus, varicose veins, oedema, pulses present
- **Musculoskeletal leg pain** – pain at rest as well as on movement, no vascular risk factors
- **Deep vein thrombosis** – acute swelling, warmth, positive ultrasound, ABPI not relevant
- **Acute compartment syndrome** – post-trauma/ischaemia, tense compartment, severe pain on passive stretch
- **Splitter:** **Does the pain resolve within 2–3 minutes of rest?** Yes → PAD. No → neurogenic claudication (needs longer to settle). Plus ABPI < 0.9 confirms PAD.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI): >1.4 suggests non-compressible (often diabetes); 0.9–1.4 normal; 0.5–0.9 claudication; < 0.5 CLI; acute limb ischaemia: often inaudible doppler signal
  - Toe pressures / transcutaneous oxygen (TCPO2) if ABPI unreliable (e.g. diabetes)
  - ECG (look for atrial fibrillation as embolic source)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, eGFR, LFTs, lipid profile, HbA1c, coagulation (if anticoagulation considered)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Duplex ultrasound** – localises stenosis/occlusion, guides intervention
  - Second-line: CT angiography (CTA) – for surgical/endovascular planning; best for multi-level disease
  - MR angiography (if contra-indication to CT contrast)
- **Special tests:**
  - Treadmill exercise test for claudication distance (if needed)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Rutherford classification** (0 = asymptomatic, 1–3 = claudication, 4 = rest pain, 5–6 = minor/major tissue loss)
  - **WIfI** (Wound, Ischaemia, foot Infection) for CLI staging
- **Never-miss test:** **ABPI + duplex ultrasound** in any patient > 50 with leg pain or non-healing ulcer – missing PAD means missing risk of MI/stroke.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Acute limb ischaemia:**
    - Immediate IV heparin (5000 IU bolus then 1000 IU/hour, check local protocol) – do not wait for imaging if pulses absent
    - **Urgent vascular surgeon review** – time is muscle
    - NBM, IV fluids, analgesia, ECG, bloods (including troponin)
    - If viable (no motor loss): urgent CTA; if non-viable (paralysis > 4hrs): amputation likely
  - **Chronic symptomatic PAD:** no acute rush
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start antiplatelet (clopidogrel 75 mg OD preferred, or aspirin 75 mg OD)
  - High-intensity statin: atorvastatin 80 mg OD (target LDL < 1.4 mmol/L)
  - Optimise blood pressure (target < 140/90, or < 130/80 if diabetes/chronic kidney disease)
  - Diabetes control
  - Smoking cessation advice + referral to cessation service
  - Supervised exercise programme referral (NICE recommends 3 months)
  - Wound care (if ulcer: keep clean, dry, non-compressive dressings; refer to podiatry)
  - Consider revascularisation: endovascular (angioplasty/stent) or surgical (bypass) depending on anatomy
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Continue antiplatelet + statin + risk factor management
  - Foot care advice: daily inspection, moisturise, avoid trauma, appropriate footwear
  - Repeat ABPI at 6–12 months or if symptoms worsen
  - Surveillance if bypass/stent – duplex ultrasound at 1, 6, 12 months then yearly
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV normal saline 1 L over 8h (if NBM for procedure) or oral hydration
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (provided not anticoagulated already)
  - Diet: NBM if planned angiogram/surgery; otherwise normal diet
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2; if acute ischaemia, hourly for first 6h

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Clopidogrel:** 75 mg PO OD (first line for symptomatic PAD)
  - **Aspirin:** 75 mg PO OD (if clopidogrel not tolerated)
  - **Atorvastatin:** 80 mg PO OD (high intensity, regardless of baseline LDL)
  - **Cilostazol:** 100 mg PO BD – for claudication when symptoms persist despite exercise, only if no heart failure (contraindicated in HF). Dose: 100 mg BD
  - **Naftidrofuryl oxalate:** 200 mg PO TDS – alternative for claudication if cilostazol not suitable
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BD + aspirin 100 mg OD (for high-risk patients, post-revascularisation? check local policy)
  - Anti-hypertensives: ACEi (ramipril 2.5-10 mg OD) or ARB if ACEi intolerant
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Cilostazol: contraindicated in heart failure (any NYHA class)
  - Clopidogrel + PPI (esomeprazole) – weak interaction; use pantoprazole if needed
  - Statins: avoid in severe liver disease; check LFTs before start
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Atorvastatin: no dose adjustment in mild-moderate renal impairment; caution if eGFR < 30
  - Clopidogrel: no adjustment
  - Cilostazol: avoid if eGFR < 25 mL/min
  - [check local policy] for heparin dosing in renal impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Heparin in acute limb ischaemia:** forgetting to give the bolus – leads to extension of thrombosis. Always check aPTT 6 hours after start. If critical limb ischaemia, consider weight-based dosing. Also **heparin + antiplatelet** – risk of bleeding; monitor for haematomas.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Acute limb ischaemia** (embolus/thrombus) – risk of amputation, compartment syndrome, reperfusion injury (hyperkalaemia, acidosis, myoglobinuria – treat with fluids, sodium bicarbonate, mannitol per local protocol)
  - **Rhabdomyolysis** from severe ischaemia – check CK, urine myoglobin
  - **Bleeding** from antiplatelet/anticoagulation (especially if dual therapy)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Non-healing ulcer / gangrene** – amputation (major/minor)
  - **Cardiovascular events** (MI, stroke, CV death) – PAD is a strong marker of systemic atherosclerosis
  - **Impotence** (aorto-iliac disease)
  - **Infection** in ischaemic ulcer (cellulitis, osteomyelitis)

### Prognosis
- 5-year risk of major cardiovascular events (MI/stroke) is 20-30% in symptomatic PAD. Up to 30% of those with critical limb ischaemia undergo major amputation within 1 year. Risk factor modification + revascularisation reduce amputation risk.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always calculate ABPI yourself at the bedside** – the most accessible test.
- **Check for atrial fibrillation** in all acute limb ischaemia – embolic source.
- **Refer all smokers to a smoking cessation service** – single best intervention for symptom progression.
- **Ensure foot inspection daily** – especially in diabetes and end-stage PAD.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not prescribe compression stockings** in arterial disease – they worsen ischaemia.
- **Do not assume leg pain is arthritis** in an elderly smoker with diabetes – check ABPI.
- **Do not delay heparin in acute limb ischaemia** while waiting for imaging – muscle necrosis begins within 4-6 hours.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden worsening of leg pain at rest
  - Leg becomes cold, pale, or numb
  - New foot ulcer develops or existing ulcer enlarges / becomes black
  - Chest pain, breathlessness, or syncope (possible MI)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Vascular clinic within 2 weeks for claudication (or 1 week for CLI)
  - GP for cardiovascular risk review (lipids, BP, glucose) in 4 weeks
  - Supervised exercise programme referral (if applicable)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / vascular registrar immediately if:**
  - Acute limb ischaemia with motor loss (paralysis)
  - NEWS2 > 5 or lactate > 4 mmol/L
  - Suspected ruptured AAA (shock + pulsatile mass)
  - Compartment syndrome (tense leg, pain on passive stretch, elevated compartment pressure)
  - Any significant bleeding (e.g. retroperitoneal haematoma after intervention)
- **ICU criteria:** critical ischaemia with multi-organ failure, severe reperfusion syndrome, or post-major amputation with haemodynamic instability.
